Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We need to group events that belong to the same patient. The patient attributes: age, gender, and possibly other info like insurance, triage_code, diagnosis, etc. However, note that patient age is given as a number; its unlikely that two different patients have exactly the same age and gender? Possibly, but we can consider that as a strong indicator.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: For a patient, the activities follow a logical flow. For example, a patient who registers in ER, then gets triage, then doctor consultation, then X-ray, then diagnosis, then discharge. That seems like a typical ER journey for patient 1 (age45 M). For patient 2 (age62 F): register in ER, triage, lab test requested, lab test completed, doctor consultation (diagnosis minor infection), prescription, discharge. Thats also a typical ER journey. For patient 3 (age28 M): register in Surgery department, doctor consultation (scheduled knee surgery), surgery preparation, surgery performed, recovery room, discharge. Thats a surgical journey.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Thus we have three distinct cases based on age and gender. However, we should also consider that there might be more subtle clues: The resources and departments might also be consistent across a patients journey? Not necessarily, but they can help. Case 1: events 1,4,7,12,15; Case 2: events 2,5,8,10,11,14,17; Case 3: events 3,6,9,13,16,18.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Event 4: Initial triage in ER, age45 M, triage_code=3. Event 7: X-ray imaging, age45 M. Event 12: Doctor consultation in ER, age45 M, diagnosis=hairline_fracture. Event 15: Discharge, age45 M. So thats consistent. Event 5: Initial triage, age62 F. Event 8: Lab test requested, age62 F. Event 10: Lab test completed, age62 F. Event 11: Doctor consultation, age62 F, diagnosis=minor_infection. Event 14: Prescription created, age62 F. Event 17: Discharge, age62 F. So consistent.

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: For patient 45 M: resources: Admin Clerk (ER), Nurse Joy (ER), Radiology Tech (Radiology), Dr. Lee (ER), Ward Clerk (ER). For patient 62 F: Admin Clerk (ER), Nurse Mark (ER), Dr. Smith (ER), Lab Technician (Laboratory), Dr. Smith (ER), Ward Clerk (ER). For patient 28 M: Admin Clerk (Surgery), Dr. Lee (Surgery), Surgical Nurse (Surgery), Dr. Lee (Surgery), Recovery Nurse (Surgery), Ward Clerk (Surgery). So each patients events are mostly within the same department (ER for first two, Surgery for third) except for the X-ray and lab events which are external departments but still associated with the same patient because of age/gender.

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: But we need to be careful: Could there be any cross-case contamination? For example, event 12: Doctor consultation, Dr. Lee, ER, age45 M, diagnosis=hairline_fracture. That is likely for patient 45 M. Event 6: Doctor consultation, Dr. Lee, Surgery, age28 M, reason=scheduled_knee_surgery. So Dr. Lee appears in both cases, but thats okay; a doctor can treat multiple patients.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets double-check each events attributes: Event 1: Register patient, ER, age45 M. This is likely start of case 1. Event 2: Register patient, ER, age62 F. Start of case 2. Event 3: Register patient, Surgery, age28 M. Start of case 3. ... All consistent.
